title: Air-source versus ground-source heat pumps
subtitle: Efficiency, installation, and which makes sense for a UK home
excerpt: Air-source heat pumps are significantly cheaper to install than ground-source but slightly less efficient in cold weather. For most UK properties, air-source is the practical choice; ground-source is suited to rural sites with sufficient land.
sourceNotes: Heat Pump Association UK performance data. gov.uk BUS scheme guidance for eligible technologies. CIBSE AM15 guidance on heat pump performance in UK climate conditions.

glossaryTerms.definition: A device that moves heat from a low-temperature source (outside air or the ground) to a higher-temperature heat emitter circuit using a refrigerant cycle and electrical energy. At a flow temperature of 35 degrees C an air-source heat pump typically delivers 3-4 kWh of heat for every 1 kWh of electricity used.

Seasonal Coefficient of Performance: the average heat output per unit of electricity consumed over a full heating season, accounting for temperature variation and defrost cycles. A more realistic performance figure than instantaneous COP.
Coefficient of Performance: the ratio of heat delivered to electricity consumed at a given operating point. COP is a snapshot figure; SCOP is the seasonal average over a full heating season.

content.text: Both air-source and ground-source
heat pumps

extract heat from a natural source and upgrade it to a useful temperature for space heating and hot water. The source differs: air-source uses the ambient outdoor air; ground-source uses the relatively stable temperature of the ground at 1-2 metres depth. The refrigerant cycle and the distribution system are the same in both types.
Efficiency comparison
A
SCOP
is the standard metric for comparing heat pump performance over a heating season. Measured SCOP data from UK homes:
Air-source heat pumps in UK residential use: median SCOP of 2.8-3.2 (Electrification of Heat project, 2022 data). In well-insulated homes running at low flow temperatures, SCOP reaches 3.5-4.0.

Ground-source heat pumps in UK residential use: median SCOP of 3.3-3.8 (same dataset). The ground temperature at slinky depth stays at 8-12 degrees C year-round, giving a consistently higher source temperature than air in winter.
The efficiency advantage of ground-source is most significant on the coldest design days. When outdoor air falls to -3 degrees C, an air-source unit's instantaneous
COP

may drop to 2.0-2.5. The ground at the same time is still at 8-10 degrees C, and a ground-source unit maintains COP of 3.0-3.5. In a UK climate with relatively few days below -5 degrees C, this design-day difference has a modest effect on the annual SCOP figure.
Installation comparison
Air-source
Outdoor unit sits on the ground outside the building or on a wall bracket. Requires a clear space of roughly 1.5 m x 0.7 m x 0.8 m for the outdoor unit.
Pipes from the outdoor unit enter the building through a 65-100 mm core drilled in the wall.
No ground works required. Installation is typically completed in one or two days by an MCS-registered installer.
Installation cost: typically 8,000-12,000 pounds for a 5-8 kW residential unit, plus the BUS grant of 7,500 pounds reduces the net cost to 500-4,500 pounds (England and Wales, 2026 figures).
Ground-source

Horizontal slinky loop requires 200-400 m2 of accessible ground at 1-1.5 m depth. The ground works take two to three days and leave the garden unusable for one to two years while the grass re-establishes.

Vertical borehole loop requires drilling to 80-120 metres depth per borehole. A 5-6 kW system typically needs two boreholes. Borehole drilling requires specialist equipment and access for a drilling rig.
Installation cost: typically 15,000-25,000 pounds for the full system including ground works. The BUS grant of 7,500 pounds applies here too, but covers a smaller proportion of the total cost.
Noise and planning

Air-source units produce noise from the outdoor fan, typically 40-50 dB at 1 m. Planning permission for an air-source heat pump in permitted development does not require prior approval in most cases, provided the unit meets the MCS noise siting rules (no closer than 1 m to the property boundary and not on a listed building). Ground-source units produce no outdoor noise; the indoor unit is similar in noise level to a refrigerator.
Which to choose
Urban or suburban property with a small garden: air-source. Ground-source is not practical without a significant garden or borehole access.
Rural property with at least 400 m2 of accessible flat land: ground-source is viable. The higher SCOP gives better running efficiency over the life of the system.

Budget constrained: air-source. The lower installation cost and the BUS grant make the net outlay substantially lower, and in a well-insulated UK home the SCOP difference rarely justifies the additional cost of ground-source.
